metadata.context: Serverless Framework plugin providing higher-level AWS constructs.

top_gaps.title: Audit Logging
top_gaps.gap: No CloudTrail or audit logging configuration found in the library
top_gaps.impact: Security compliance gap — consumers cannot verify audit trail setup through the plugin

top_gaps.title: API Versioning Strategy
top_gaps.gap: No versioning strategy for the plugin's public API surface
top_gaps.impact: Breaking changes to construct interfaces affect all consumers without a migration path

top_gaps.title: Stored Procedures and Schema Complexity
top_gaps.gap: DynamoDB construct uses a rigid single-table schema with hard-coded attribute names
top_gaps.impact: Couples consumer applications to specific data modeling patterns without escape hatch

top_gaps.title: Distributed Tracing
top_gaps.gap: No tracing instrumentation in any CDK constructs generated by the plugin
top_gaps.impact: Consumers of Lift constructs do not get tracing propagation built into their infrastructure

top_gaps.title: Centralized Identity Integration
top_gaps.gap: No Cognito or centralized identity construct available for consumers
top_gaps.impact: Consumers must configure identity management entirely outside Lift

quick_agent_wins.name: RAG-based Knowledge Agent
quick_agent_wins.prerequisite: Documentation exists — 13 Markdown docs covering each construct type plus README with usage examples
quick_agent_wins.what_it_enables: A knowledge agent could index the Lift documentation to answer developer questions about construct configuration, usage patterns, and troubleshooting
quick_agent_wins.additional_steps: Documentation could be augmented with troubleshooting guides and FAQ content to improve retrieval quality

quick_agent_wins.name: DevOps Agent
quick_agent_wins.prerequisite: CI/CD pipeline exists — 3 GitHub Actions workflows with automated test, lint, type-check, and release stages
quick_agent_wins.what_it_enables: An agent that monitors CI status, triggers releases, and manages the npm publish workflow
quick_agent_wins.additional_steps: Add API-accessible build status endpoints or webhook integrations for the agent to consume

findings.title: No audit logging configuration in generated infrastructure
findings.description: No CloudTrail configuration or audit logging infrastructure is generated by any Lift construct. The plugin does not provision CloudTrail trails, CloudWatch log groups for audit purposes, or any logging infrastructure.
findings.gap: The library generates infrastructure (DynamoDB tables, S3 buckets, SQS queues) without accompanying audit logging. Consumers must separately configure CloudTrail.
findings.recommendation: Add an optional audit/logging configuration to the plugin's top-level lift config that provisions a CloudTrail trail. Alternatively, document audit logging requirements clearly.

findings.title: No API versioning strategy for construct schemas
findings.description: No explicit versioning strategy for the construct schemas (the public API surface). Breaking changes to CONSTRUCTS_DEFINITION or individual construct schemas have no migration path.
findings.gap: No deprecation warnings, schema migration utilities, or versioned construct types exist. Breaking changes silently break existing serverless.yml files.
findings.recommendation: Introduce construct schema versioning or implement a deprecation-warning mechanism. Add a CHANGELOG.md with explicit breaking-change callouts per release.

findings.title: Rigid hard-coded DynamoDB schema in construct
findings.description: The DatabaseDynamoDBSingleTable construct hard-codes a specific single-table design pattern with fixed attribute names (PK, SK), up to 5 LSIs with hard-coded sort keys, and a hard-coded TimeToLive attribute.
findings.gap: Hard-coded schema is opinionated and inflexible. Consumers who need different attribute names cannot customize without forking.
findings.recommendation: Make the DynamoDB construct's attribute names configurable via the schema definition. Allow custom partition/sort key names, TTL attribute, and LSI/GSI patterns. Keep current defaults.

findings.title: No distributed tracing in generated constructs
findings.description: The library does not instrument distributed tracing in any generated construct. No X-Ray or OpenTelemetry configuration is included in Lambda or API Gateway resources.
findings.gap: Generated Lambda functions do not enable X-Ray active tracing. API Gateway stages do not enable tracing. Consumers must manually enable tracing.
findings.recommendation: Add optional tracing configuration (e.g., lift.tracing: true) that enables X-Ray active tracing on all generated Lambda functions and API Gateway stages.

findings.title: No centralized identity construct available
findings.description: The library does not generate or integrate with any centralized identity provider. No Cognito user pool construct exists. Website constructs do not offer built-in auth integration.
findings.gap: No Cognito, identity pool, or OIDC integration available as a Lift construct. Consumers must configure identity entirely outside of Lift.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding a Cognito-based authentication construct that provisions a User Pool and integrates with API Gateway authorizers and CloudFront signed URLs.

findings.title: No secrets management enforcement or guidance
findings.description: The Stripe provider reads a secretKey from configuration without enforcing use of Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store. No runtime warning when secrets appear to be plaintext.
findings.gap: Library does not guide consumers toward proper secrets management. The Stripe secretKey could easily contain a plaintext API key.
findings.recommendation: Add documentation and validation guiding secrets through variable resolvers. Consider a runtime warning when secret-like values don't use variable references.

findings.title: TypeScript 4.x and Node 16 version lag
findings.description: The library is written in TypeScript 4.3 targeting ES2019, with Node.js 16.6.2 specified in .nvmrc. The CI matrix tests on Node 20, 22, and 24. The aws-cdk-lib ^2.215.0 provides deep AWS SDK interop. However, TypeScript 4.x is outdated (5.x is current) and aws-sdk v2 is used in devDependencies.
findings.gap: TypeScript 4.3 is behind current TypeScript 5.x. The .nvmrc specifies Node 16 (approaching EOL), though CI tests on modern Node versions. aws-sdk v2 is used in dev/test code.
findings.recommendation: Upgrade TypeScript to 5.x for improved type inference and performance. Update .nvmrc to Node 20 LTS. Migrate test code from aws-sdk v2 to @aws-sdk v3 client packages.

findings.title: Minor coupling in plugin entry point
findings.description: The library uses a well-structured modular architecture with clear separation: providers/, constructs/aws/, utils/, and a central plugin entry point. Each construct is an independent class with its own schema and lifecycle. The provider pattern acts as a factory with no circular dependencies.
findings.gap: The plugin entry point (src/plugin.ts) handles variable resolution, schema registration, and CloudFormation compilation in a single class. This coupling is manageable for a library of this size.
findings.recommendation: The current modular-monolith structure with clear interfaces is appropriate for a library/plugin. Consider extracting variable resolution into a dedicated module if the plugin grows significantly.

findings.title: S3 sync lacks timeout and progress reporting
findings.description: The library has a few potentially long-running operations: S3 sync during postDeploy, SQS message polling, and the eject command. The s3-sync and queue polling use proper async/await patterns with ora spinners for user feedback.
findings.gap: The S3 sync operation has no explicit timeout or progress reporting beyond the spinner. For very large deployments, this could block the CLI indefinitely without status updates.
findings.recommendation: Add configurable timeout to S3 sync operations and emit progress events (files synced / total) for better developer experience during long deployments.

findings.title: Implicit Vpc coupling and static construct registry
findings.description: The library implements a provider/construct registry pattern (LiftPlugin.registerProviders(), AwsProvider.registerConstructs()) as internal service discovery. Constructs are located by type string, and variables are resolved via ${construct:id.property}.
findings.gap: The Vpc construct auto-registers itself on the provider (provider.setVpcConfig()), creating an implicit coupling. Construct types are hard-coded in the AwsProvider import list with no dynamic registry.
findings.recommendation: Consider a dynamic construct registry that supports plugin-contributed constructs without modifying AwsProvider.ts imports. This would enable third-party construct packages.

findings.title: No document parsing pipeline in Storage construct
findings.description: The library provides a Storage construct that creates S3 buckets with best-practice configuration: blocked public access, configurable encryption, lifecycle rules, and archival policies. S3 is the correct managed object storage choice.
findings.gap: No parsing pipeline capabilities (Textract, document processing) are included in the Storage construct. The construct focuses on raw object storage without data extraction or indexing.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding an optional parsing configuration to the Storage construct that provisions Textract or Lambda-based parsing pipelines for common document types.

findings.title: Data access layer limited to resource references
findings.description: The library provides a centralized data access pattern through construct variables (${construct:myTable.tableName}). Each construct exposes a consistent variables() method and permissions() method that centralizes resource access.
findings.gap: The data access layer is limited to resource-reference resolution (ARNs, names). There is no abstraction over data operations themselves — consumers still write their own DynamoDB/S3 SDK calls.
findings.recommendation: The current level of abstraction (resource provisioning + IAM) is appropriate for a library that generates infrastructure. Adding data-operation abstractions would overlap with application frameworks and exceed the plugin's scope.

findings.title: CDK version not explicitly validated or documented
findings.description: The DatabaseDynamoDBSingleTable construct uses DynamoDB, a serverless managed service without engine versions. The aws-cdk-lib dependency is pinned to ^2.215.0, which is a recent release.
findings.gap: While DynamoDB itself has no EOL concerns, the aws-cdk-lib version is only floor-pinned with caret (^), meaning consumers could get different CDK versions. No explicit documentation states which CDK versions are validated.
findings.recommendation: Document the minimum validated aws-cdk-lib version in README and test against the latest CDK version in CI to catch breaking changes early.

findings.title: Webhook construct lacks built-in authorization options
findings.description: The Webhook construct generates API Gateway endpoints with configurable settings. The Stripe provider integrates with Stripe's webhook signature verification. The plugin delegates authentication to the Serverless Framework's own auth mechanisms.
findings.gap: The Webhook construct does not offer built-in authentication options (API key, JWT authorizer, IAM auth). Consumers must manually configure authorization on generated API Gateway resources via the extensions mechanism.
findings.recommendation: Add optional authorization configuration to the Webhook construct supporting API Gateway authorizer types (Cognito, Lambda authorizer, IAM).

pathways.name: Move to Cloud Native
pathways.key_trigger_criteria: Not applicable — repo_type is 'library' and the pathway requires a deployable workload

pathways.name: Move to Open Source
pathways.key_trigger_criteria: DATA-Q4 = 2 but no commercial database engines detected. Library generates DynamoDB (open-source-compatible managed service).

triggering_questions.note: DATA-Q4 < 3 but no commercial DB engines detected — contextual guard blocks trigger

pathways.not_triggered_reason: No commercial database engines (Oracle, SQL Server) detected. The library generates DynamoDB infrastructure which is an AWS-managed service, not a commercial database.

pathways.name: Move to Managed Databases
pathways.key_trigger_criteria: Not applicable — repo_type is 'library' and the pathway requires database infrastructure management

pathways.key_trigger_criteria: Not applicable — repo_type is 'library' and the pathway requires data processing workloads

pathways.name: Move to Modern DevOps
pathways.key_trigger_criteria: Not applicable — repo_type is 'library'. Libraries use build/publish pipelines, not deployment pipelines.

pathways.not_triggered_reason: No AI/agent intent detected in portfolio or service context. Context mentions 'Serverless Framework plugin providing higher-level AWS constructs' with no AI-related signal terms.
